sections.title: Set Up Your Nursing Station
sections.summary: A well-prepared nursing spot saves energy when you're feeding 8–12 times a day.
tips: Keep a large water bottle (at least 750 mL) within arm's reach — you'll be thirsty every single feed
Have snacks nearby: nuts, energy balls, or crackers you can eat one-handed
A nursing pillow (Boppy or My Brest Friend) reduces arm and back strain significantly
Dim lighting or a small lamp for night feeds — bright lights wake baby more than needed
Phone charger within reach — feeds can last 20–45 minutes
Burp cloths: keep at least 3 within reach at all times
A footstool raises your lap and reduces lower back strain during long feeds
warnings: Avoid deep, squishy sofas for newborn feeding — it's hard to maintain a safe hold if you fall asleep
Don't feed in complete darkness — you need to check latch and baby's colour

sections.title: Room Temperature & Comfort
sections.summary: Temperature affects how alert and comfortable baby is during feeds.
tips: Ideal room temperature for feeding: 20–22°C (68–72°F)
A slightly cooler room keeps baby awake enough to feed well — too warm and they fall asleep at the breast
If baby keeps dozing off mid-feed, undress them to their onesie or tickle their feet gently
Skin-to-skin contact during feeds helps regulate baby's temperature naturally
warnings: Avoid drafts or cold air directly on baby during feeds
Do not use hot packs on your breasts before feeding without checking with a lactation consultant first

sections.title: Getting a Good Latch
sections.summary: A deep latch is the single most important factor in comfortable, effective breastfeeding.
tips: Baby's mouth should cover most of the areola, not just the nipple
Nose should be clear — not smashed into the breast
Lower lip should be flanged outward (like a fish lip)
You should hear swallowing — soft 'kuh' or 'cuh' sounds every few sucks
Nipple should come out round after a feed, not pinched or lipstick-shaped
Tummy-to-tummy positioning: baby's ear, shoulder, and hip in a straight line
Try the 'sandwich' technique: compress your breast like a burger before offering it
warnings: Pain beyond initial latch-on discomfort (first 10–15 seconds) is a sign of a shallow latch — break suction and retry
To break suction safely: insert a clean finger into the corner of baby's mouth before pulling away
whenToCall: Nipple pain lasting throughout the full feed after day 5
Cracked, bleeding, or blistered nipples that aren't healing
Baby's weight not returning to birth weight by day 10–14
You suspect tongue or lip tie — a lactation consultant or pediatrician can assess

sections.title: Breastfeeding Positions
sections.summary: Different positions work better at different times — find what's comfortable for you.
tips: Cradle hold: classic position, baby across your lap facing you — good once latch is established
Cross-cradle: opposite arm supports baby's head — gives more control for newborns
Football (clutch) hold: baby tucked under your arm — great after C-section or for large breasts
Side-lying: lying on your side facing baby — great for night feeds and C-section recovery
Laid-back (biological nurturing): recline slightly and let baby lie on your chest — uses gravity to help latch
Switch positions if you have a blocked duct — different positions drain different lobes

sections.title: Feeding Frequency & Demand Feeding
sections.summary: Newborns feed frequently — this is normal and essential for supply.
tips: Feed on demand: every 2–3 hours from start of last feed (8–12 feeds per 24 hours) in the first weeks
Watch for hunger cues before crying: rooting, sucking hands, turning head side to side
Wake baby to feed if it's been 4+ hours in the first 4 weeks — they can sleep through hunger
Each breast for 10–20 minutes — offer the second side after the first is drained
Remember which side you last fed on (app tracker helps!) — alternate each feed
Cluster feeding (feeds every 45–60 minutes for hours) usually happens in evenings — this is normal and builds supply
Growth spurts happen around days 3, 7–10, 2–3 weeks, 6 weeks — expect more feeds during these times
warnings: Scheduled feeding (clock-watching) can undermine supply in the early weeks
Don't give a pacifier until breastfeeding is well established (usually 3–4 weeks)

sections.title: Burping Technique
sections.summary: Effective burping prevents gas pain and reduces spit-up.
tips: Burp between switching breasts and after the feed ends
Over-the-shoulder: baby's chin on your shoulder, pat or rub their back firmly
Sitting up: support baby's chin and chest with one hand, pat their back with the other
Tummy across lap: lay baby face-down across your lap, support their head, pat the back
If no burp in 5 minutes it's fine — not every feed produces a burp
Move baby gently to a horizontal position after a confirmed burp (keep upright 15–20 min after feeds)

sections.title: Bottle Feeding (Pumped or Formula)
sections.summary: Paced bottle feeding reduces overfeeding and keeps baby comfortable.
tips: Hold bottle horizontal (not tilted steeply) — baby should work for the milk, not have it pour in
Allow baby to latch onto the teat fully before tilting slightly
Pause every few minutes and tip the bottle down — mimic the natural pauses of breastfeeding
Watch for swallowing and pauses — never force a baby to finish a bottle
0–1 month: 60–90 mL per feed; 1–2 months: 90–120 mL — but follow baby's cues
Warm formula/breast milk to body temperature (test on wrist — not hot)
Formula: prepare fresh for each feed; don't reheat leftover formula
Breast milk: can sit at room temperature for 4 hours; fridge 4 days; freezer 6 months
warnings: Never prop a bottle — always hold it during feeds
Never microwave breast milk or formula — creates hot spots

sections.title: Building & Maintaining Milk Supply
sections.summary: Supply is demand-driven — the more you feed or pump, the more you make.
tips: Feed frequently in the first weeks — this is what establishes supply
Offer both breasts every feed for the first 4 weeks
Power pumping (pumping for 20 min, rest 10 min, pump 10 min, rest 10 min, pump 10 min) can boost supply
Stay hydrated: aim for 2.5–3L of water a day
Foods associated with supply: oats, fenugreek, brewer's yeast, flaxseed (though evidence is anecdotal)
Stress, fatigue, and dehydration are the biggest supply killers — prioritise rest
If pumping, double-pump (both sides simultaneously) is more effective than single
whenToCall: Baby losing more than 10% of birth weight
Fewer than 6 wet nappies per day after day 4
Baby still not back to birth weight by day 14
Breasts never feel full or never let down

sections.title: Engorgement
sections.summary: Milk coming in on days 3–5 can cause painful swelling — this is temporary.
tips: Feed frequently (at least every 2–3 hours) to keep milk moving
Cold cabbage leaves in your bra between feeds can relieve discomfort — change every 20–30 min
Cold packs (wrapped in cloth) for 15–20 minutes between feeds reduces inflammation
Warm shower or warm compress just before a feed to help with let-down
Gentle breast massage or hand expression before feeding softens the areola so baby can latch
Avoid over-pumping to 'relieve' engorgement — this signals your body to make more milk
warnings: Engorgement should improve significantly after 24–48 hours of frequent feeding
If engorgement is accompanied by fever (38°C+), redness, and flu-like symptoms — this may be mastitis
whenToCall: Fever over 38°C with a hard, red, painful area of the breast
Engorgement not improving after 48 hours of frequent feeding

sections.title: Blocked Ducts & Mastitis
sections.summary: A hard lump in the breast can be a blocked duct — caught early it resolves without antibiotics.
tips: Continue breastfeeding frequently — stopping makes it worse
Position baby's chin toward the blocked area to help drain that lobe
Gentle massage toward the nipple while feeding or in a warm shower
Heat before feeds, cold after feeds
Rest as much as possible — your immune system needs it
warnings: A blocked duct becomes mastitis if the area becomes red, hot, and swollen, with flu-like symptoms and fever
Do not stop breastfeeding with mastitis — continuing to feed helps clear the infection
whenToCall: Blocked duct not resolving in 24–48 hours
Mastitis symptoms — you may need antibiotics (safe to take while breastfeeding)

sections.title: Reading Hunger & Fullness Cues
sections.summary: Crying is a late hunger cue — learning earlier signals makes feeds calmer.
tips: Early hunger: rooting (turning head side to side), sucking on hands or tongue, increased alertness
Active hunger: opening mouth, pulling legs up, fussing or squirming
Late hunger: crying — a crying baby is harder to latch
If baby is crying from hunger, try skin-to-skin first to calm them before attempting to latch
Fullness cues: releasing the nipple, turning head away, hands relaxed and open, falling asleep
Don't force a baby to take more if they've shown fullness cues

sections.title: Sore Nipples & Pain
sections.summary: Some tenderness is normal in the first 1–2 weeks, but sharp pain throughout a feed is not.
tips: Express a few drops of breast milk after each feed and air dry — natural antibacterial properties promote healing
Lanolin cream (like Lansinoh) applied after feeds soothes cracked nipples
Medical-grade silicone nipple shields can give relief while healing — use temporarily with LC guidance
Gel pads (hydrogel) provide cooling relief between feeds
Ensure your nursing bra is not too tight — cutting off circulation slows healing
Let nipples air dry when possible
warnings: White or shiny nipple tips after feeding can indicate Raynaud's phenomenon or vasospasm — treatable
Burning, itching, or stabbing pain through the breast after feeds may indicate thrush — both you and baby need treatment
whenToCall: Cracked or bleeding nipples not healing after a week
Symptoms of thrush: burning pain, shiny/flaky/itchy nipples, white patches in baby's mouth

sections.title: How Much Milk by Age
sections.summary: A quick reference for typical feeding volumes and frequency — use as a guide, not a rule.
tips: Newborn (0–2 weeks): 8–12 feeds per day; 15–30 mL (0.5–1 oz) per feed, increasing to 60–90 mL by end of week 2
2–4 weeks: 8–10 feeds per day; 60–90 mL (2–3 oz) per bottle feed
1–2 months: 7–9 feeds per day; 90–120 mL (3–4 oz) per bottle feed; breastfed babies nurse 8+ times
2–3 months: 6–8 feeds per day; 120–150 mL (4–5 oz) per bottle feed
3–4 months: 6–7 feeds per day; 120–180 mL (4–6 oz) per bottle feed; daily total roughly 750–950 mL (25–32 oz)
Breastfed babies: you can't measure volume — use output (6+ wet nappies/day) and weight gain as your guide
Formula-fed rule of thumb: 150 mL per kg of body weight per day (e.g. 4 kg baby = ~600 mL/day total)
warnings: These are averages — a healthy baby may eat more or less. Follow hunger and fullness cues, not the clock or volume
Never force a baby to finish a bottle — overfeeding formula-fed babies is a real risk
whenToCall: Consistently eating significantly less than the lower end of ranges AND not gaining weight
Consistently refusing feeds or gagging/vomiting after most feeds

sections.title: Feed on Wake (Not to Sleep)
sections.summary: Feeding at the start of an awake period — not at the end — helps prevent feeding becoming the only way baby can fall asleep.
tips: The Eat-Play-Sleep approach: when baby wakes, feed first, then play, then sleep — in that order
Feeding at wake means baby is hungry and alert enough to feed well, rather than half-asleep and snacking
Separating the feed from sleep onset reduces the chance of baby needing to feed to fall back asleep overnight
This approach works best from around 3–4 weeks — in the first 2 weeks, newborns eat and sleep on demand regardless
It's fine for the feed to take a while — the 'play' phase can be brief (a nappy change + 10 min of face time is plenty for a newborn)
If baby falls asleep at the breast at the end of a feed, that's OK — the goal is not to avoid all sleep-associations, just to shift the feed earlier in the cycle
warnings: Don't apply this rigidly in the first 2–3 weeks — newborns need to feed whenever they show hunger cues
Cluster feeding periods (evenings) will disrupt any schedule — this is normal, go with the flow

sections.title: Preparing for Solids (3–4 Months)
sections.summary: Solids start around 6 months — but you can lay the groundwork earlier.
tips: Bring baby to the table during family mealtimes — let them observe eating from around 3 months
Talk about food: describe what you're eating and the smells, colours, and textures
Floor play and tummy time build the core and neck strength needed for sitting up for solids
Look for readiness signs (not before 6 months): sitting with minimal support, loss of tongue-thrust reflex, showing interest in food by watching and reaching
WHO and most paediatric bodies recommend exclusive breastfeeding or formula to 6 months — no solids, water, or juice before then
Starting solids doesn't replace milk feeds at first — milk remains the primary nutrition source until 12 months
